<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Nigihayahi]][[fr:Nigihayahi]][[ja:ニギハヤヒ]][[simple:Nigihayahi]][[zh:饒速日命]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Nigihayahi]][[fr:Nigihayahi]][[ja:ニギハヤヒ]][[simple:Nigihayahi]][[zh:饒速日命]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Nigihayahi]][[ja:ニギハヤヒ]][[simple:Nigihayahi]][[zh:饒速日命]]






{{Expand Japanese|ニギハヤヒ}}
{{Infobox deity
| type = Shinto
| image = File:饒速日尊の墳墓.jpg
| caption = Tomb of Nigihayahi
| father = {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-oshihomimi|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}}<ref name="kokugakuin" />
|spouse={{ill|Mikashikiya-hime|ja|ミカシキヤヒメ}}| gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}
| parents ={{ill|Oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}}; {{ill|Takuhata Chijihime no Mikoto|WD=Q11110848|1=Takuhadachiji-hime}}| consort = {{ill|Mikashikiyahime|WD=Q11341880|1=Mikashikiya-hime}}
| offspring = {{ill|Umashimaji|WD=Q10946169|1=Umashimaji}}
| equivalent5_type = Japanese | equivalent5 = {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|WD=Q506610|1=Ajisukitakahikone}}; {{ill|Ame no Wakahiko|WD=Q10940674|1=Ame no Wakahiko}}; {{ill|Hitokotonushi|WD=Q10864079|1=Hitokotonushi}}; {{ill|Hoderi|WD=Q2716491|1=Hoderi}}; {{ill|Honoakari|WD=Q60673696|1=Honoakari}}; {{ill|Honosusori|WD=Q10731395|1=Hosuseri}}; {{ill|Isetsuhiko|WD=Q17193127|1=Isetsuhiko}}; {{ill|Kotoshironushi|WD=Q8009611|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}}; {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|WD=Q3090037|1=Sarutahiko Ōkami}}; {{ill|Yamato no ōkuni tama no kami|WD=Q11387080|1=Yamato Okunitama}}; {{ill|honoakari|WD=Q10940411|1=Amenohoakari}}; {{ill|Ōmononushi|WD=Q10936701|1=Ōmononushi}}; {{ill|Ōyamakui|WD=Q10935105|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}
}}

'''Nigihayahi''' is a Japanese deity.<ref name="kokugakuin">{{Cite web |date=2023-03-21 |title=Nigihayahi {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9356 |access-date=2023-12-05 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165541/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9356 |archive-date=2023-03-21 }}</ref> mentioned in the [[Kojiki]], {{ill|Kujiki|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujiki|qq=}}, and [[Nihon Shoki]]<ref name="Como-2009a">{{Cite book|last=Como |first=Michael |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=-UNEhdX6ePMC&dq=nigihayahi&pg=PA169 |title=Weaving and Binding: Immigrant Gods and Female Immortals in Ancient Japan |date=2009-09-02 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-2957-5 |language=en|qq=draft}}</ref>

He was involved with the [[Tenson kōrin|Tenson Korin]].<ref name="Satomi-2013a">{{Cite book |last=Satomi |first=Kishio |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uWem9l1u-9cC&dq=nigihayahi&pg=PA109 |title=Discovery of Japanese Idealism |date=2013-07-04 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-39365-5 |language=en}}</ref> The [[Mononobe clan]] claimed him as their ancestor.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&dq=nigihayahi&pg=PA159 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |language=en}}</ref>

In the {{ill|Kujiki|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujiki|qq=}}, {{ill|Amenohoakari|simple|User:Immanuelle/Amenohoakari|fr|User:Immanuelle/Amenohoakari|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amenohoakari|qq=}} is considered the same ''kami'' as {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|ニギハヤヒ|fr|6=en:draft|8=simple|9=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi|qq=}}, the ancestral god of the Hozumi and [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] clans, but this contradicts their generational relationships and areas of activity.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Hoga |first=Toshio |title=Shoki Mononobe-uji no Keifu (1) Kokigi no Heya (初期物部氏の系譜 (1) 古樹紀之房間) |year=2007 |location=Japan}}</ref>

He is sometimes described as the older brother of [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]]<ref>{{Cite book |last=Barnes |first=Gina |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=tn1IpE-YZOQC&dq=nigihayahi&pg=PA153 |title=State Formation in Japan: Emergence of a 4th-Century Ruling Elite |date=2007-03-12 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-134-38469-3 |language=en}}</ref>, or the son of {{ill|Ame-no-oshihomimi|en|Ame-no-oshihomimi|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame-no-oshihomimi|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}}.<ref name="kokugakuin" />

The nature of the deity and its myths in the [[Kojiki]], {{ill|Kujiki|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujiki|qq=}}, and [[Nihon Shoki]] all suggest a possible Korean influence.<ref name="Como-2009a" />

== Jimmu's Eastern Expedition ==
During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] Jimmu and his brothers reached Naniwa (modern-day {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}}), they encountered another local chieftain, ''Nagasunehiko'' ("the long-legged man"), and [[Itsuse no Mikoto|Itsuse]] was killed in the ensuing battle<ref>{{Cite book |last=Bunko (Japan) |first=Tōyō |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sZnWAAAAMAAJ&q=%22Itsuse%22 |title=Memoirs of the Research Department |date=1975 |language=en|qq=draft}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |date=2023-03-21 |title=Kamuyamatoiwarebiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9379 |access-date=2023-11-23 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230321165628/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9379 |archive-date=2023-03-21 }}</ref> .<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Monbushō |first1=Japan |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wXoLAAAAIAAJ&dq=%22Itsuse%22&pg=PA24 |title=History of the Empire of Japan |last2=Brinkley |first2=FRank |date=1893 |publisher=Dai Nippon Tosho Kabushiki Kwaisha, by order of the Department of Education. Printed at the Japan Mail Office, Yokohama |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Roberts |first=Jeremy |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&dq=%22Itsuse%22&pg=PA63 |title=Japanese Mythology A to Z |date=2009 |publisher=Infobase Publishing |isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Anonymous |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=YDXaEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Itsuse%22&pg=PA148 |title=The Great Events: Vol. 1 |date=2023-09-29 |publisher=BoD – Books on Demand |isbn=978-3-368-19823-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Weiss |first=David |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nLnMEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22Itsuse%22&pg=PA99 |title=The God Susanoo and Korea in Japan's Cultural Memory: Ancient Myths and Modern Empire |date=2022-01-13 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing |isbn=978-1-350-27119-7 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=James |first=David H. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=q540U2rrWKcC&dq=%22Itsuse%22&pg=PA363 |title=The Rise and Fall of the Japanese Empire |date=2010-11-01 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-136-92547-4 |language=en}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Various |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=P1mLEAAAQBAJ&dq=nigihayahi&pg=PT19 |title=Japan: From the Japanese Government History |date=2022-09-16 |publisher=DigiCat |language=en}}</ref> Nagasunehiko claimed to follow Nigihayahi.<ref name="kokugakuin" /><ref name=":0" /> Jimmu realized that they had been defeated because they battled eastward against the sun, so he decided to land on the east side of [[Kii Peninsula]] and to battle westward. They reached [[Kumano Region|Kumano]], and, with the guidance of a {{ill|Three-legged crow|en|Three-legged crow|WD=Q1323130|lt=Three-legged crow|1=Three-legged crow}}, ''Yatagarasu'' ("eight-span crow"), they moved to {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato|1=Yamato Province}}. There, they once again battled Nagasunehiko and were victorious.<ref name="Iwate">{{cite web |date=September 24, 2004 |script-title=ja:朝廷軍の侵略に抵抗 |url=http://www.iwate-np.co.jp/sekai/sekaiisan/sekaiisan6.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160303180753/http://www.iwate-np.co.jp/sekai/sekaiisan/sekaiisan6.htm |archive-date=March 3, 2016 |access-date=March 1, 2011 |publisher=[[Iwate Nippo]] |language=ja}}</ref> Nigihayahi killed Nagasunehiko and submitted to Jimmu.<ref name="kokugakuin" /><ref name=":0" />

In Yamato, [[Nigihayahi]], who also claimed descent from the Takamagahara gods, was protected by Nagasunehiko. However, when Nigihayahi met Jimmu, he accepted Jimmu's legitimacy. At this point, Jimmu is said to have ascended to the throne of Japan.<ref>[https://www.kusuyama.jp/culture/tombo-dragonfly/ メンテナンス中]</ref>

== In popular culture ==
He is present in {{ill|Ghost of Tsushima|en|Ghost of Tsushima|WD=Q42564798|lt=Ghost of Tsushima|1=Ghost of Tsushima}}.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-08-21 |title=Charm Of Nigihayahi-No-Mikoto - Location & Effects {{!}} Ghost Of Tsushima |url=https://gamewith.net/ghost-of-tsushima/article/show/20521 |access-date=2023-12-05 |website=GameWith |language=en-US}}</ref>

==References==
{{reflist}}
{{User:Immanuelle/Isonokami Faith}}

{{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}}
<!--removing this template because now we do not need it. All everybodywiki edit histories have been imported-->

{{translated page|ja|ニギハヤヒ|version=103408922|comment=Imported full ja history}}


{{everybodywiki link|Nigihayahi}}

{{everybodywiki link|Nigihayahi|Nigihayahi}}

[[fr:Nigihayahi]]
[[ja:ニギハヤヒ]]
[[zh:饒速日命]]

[[ja:Nigihayahi]]

<!--all this actually mandates is a completion tier 7 ranking. We are not using the promising draft template here. If I get anywhere near implementing it, I will do so based on the actual completion tiers of this wiki rather than whether I added them when they were on simple english wikipedia or wherever--><!-- Bot generated title --><!--Nigihayahi--><!--Nigihayahi--><!--Nigihayahi--><!--Nigihayahi--><!--Nigihayahi--><!--Nigihayahi-->
[[en:Nigihayahi]]<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->
<!--zhwiki derived wikidata interwikis-->
{{wikidata link|Q10949054}}
[[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]]
[[Category:History of Nara Prefecture]]
[[Category:Japanese deities]]
[[Category:Mononobe clan]]
[[Category:People of Yamato Province]]
[[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
[[Category:Need translation]]
